coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of highly efficient fluorescent silicon quantum dots used for highly sensitive sensor of tetracycline in honey samples and antibacterial agent
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Shuchen
Hou, Xin
Chi, Yuting
Sun, Wanlin
Chen, Fang
Luo, Kang
Chai, Shuiqin
coredata.dc:description: The excessive use of antibiotics and the presence of tetracycline (TC) residues poses a significant threat to human health. It is imperative to develop antibacterial materials which address the issue of bacterial resistance as well as to establish reliable methods for detecting TC to ensure food safety. This study reports the synthesis of silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) with blue fluorescence emission at 420 nm via a hydrothermal method, using allyloxytrimethylsilane, triacetoxy(methyl)silane as the silicon sources and sodium citrate as a reducing agent. The synthesized SiQDs demonstrated remarkable photostability in 60 min of UV irradiation as well as pH-stability across a range from 2.09 to 11.92. They exhibited salt tolerance and anti-oxidant capacity, even when the concentrations of NaCl and H2O2 were up to 4 mol/L and 1000 μmol/L, respectively. Notably, the SiQDs displayed efficient antibacterial activity without resistance by electrostatic interaction and excessive production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which would damage the bacterial cell walls and subsequently inhibit bacterial growth and reproduction. The minimum inhibitory concentration of SiQDs was 0.45 mg/mL against Escherichia coli and 0.25 mg/mL against Staphylococcus aureus, respectively. Besides, a fluorescence nanoprobe based on SiQDs was meaningfully constructed to sensitively and rapidly determine TC owing to the internal filtration effect. The limit of detection for SiQDs to TC was 0.0006 μmol/L with a linear range from 0.001 to 0.010 μmol/L. The probe's practicality was confirmed in spiked honey samples from different manufacturers with an approximate 100 % recovery of TC. Consequently, this research presents a promising avenue for enhancing the detection of TC in food safety and the development of antibacterial agents without bacterial resistance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon quantum dots
Antibacterial activity
Tetracycline detection
No resistance

Detection of TC by the SiQDs
Application of TC in actual samples

This research was funded by Chongqing Three Gorges Medical College 2023 University-level Natural Science Project (Grant No. XJ2023000204).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of SiQDs. (a) TEM images, inset: HRTEM images; (b) Size distribution; (c) The full-scan spectra of XPS; (d) FTIR spectra; (e) UV–vis spectra; (f) Fluorescence spectra.

The FL intensity of SiQDs with different interfering substance: (a) amino acids and antibiotics (1: Blank, 2: Tetracycline, 3: Glucose, 4: Metronidazole, 5: Chloramphenicol, 6: Roxithromycin, 7: Amoxicillin, 8: Furazolidone, 9: Streptomycin, 10: Ciprofloxacin, 11: Doxycycline, 12: Arginine, 13: Alanine, 14: Lysine, 15: Glycine, 16: Leucine, 17: Phenylalanine, 18: Valine, 19: Tryptophan, 20: Proline); (b) anion and cation (21: Blank, 22: TC, 23:C2H3O2 −, 24: PO4 3−, 25: F−, 26: NO3 −, 27: IO3 −, 28: Cl−, 29: S2−, 30: Br−, 31: H2PO4 −, 32: HPO4 2−, 33: CN−, 34: Cu2+, 35: Al3+, 36: K+, 37: Sn2+, 38: Ba2+, 39: Ca2+, 40: Na+, 41: NH4 +,42: Co2+, 43: Fe3+, 44: Mg2+, 45: Zn2+); (c) The change trend of the FL intensity at different TC concentrations; (d) The FL intensity of SiQDs at different TC concentrations (0–100 μmol/L), the inset illustrated the linear relationship between (F 0
F)/F 0 and the concentration of TC. Error bars showed the standard deviation of three experiments (n = 3).

(a) Fluorescence lifetime decay of SiQDs in the absence (orange) and presence (green) of TC. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of TC, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of SiQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The antibacterial activity of SiQDs on the glass plate; (b) The inhibition curve against E.coli; (c) The inhibition curve against S. aureus; (d) The drug resistance of SiQDs. Error bars showed the standard deviation of three experiments (n = 3).
SEM microscopic images of (i) E. coli and (iii) S. aureus before being treated, (ii) E. coli and (iv) S. aureus after being treated with SiQDs.
The Zeta potential (a) and generated superoxide (b) of SiQDs. Error bars showed the standard deviation of three experiments (n = 3).
Illustration for synthesis, antibacterial and detection properties of SiQDs.
Detection of TC in the honey samples.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of highly efficient fluorescent silicon quantum dots used for highly sensitive sensor of tetracycline in honey samples and antibacterial agent

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Chongqing University of Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: People's Hospital Affiliated to Chongqing Three Gorges Medical College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: People's Hospital Affiliated to Chongqing Three Gorges Medical College, Chongqing 404037, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The excessive use of antibiotics and the presence of tetracycline (TC) residues poses a significant threat to human health. It is imperative to develop antibacterial materials which address the issue of bacterial resistance as well as to establish reliable methods for detecting TC to ensure food safety. This study reports the synthesis of silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) with blue fluorescence emission at 420 nm via a hydrothermal method, using allyloxytrimethylsilane, triacetoxy(methyl)silane as the silicon sources and sodium citrate as a reducing agent. The synthesized SiQDs demonstrated remarkable photostability in 60 min of UV irradiation as well as pH-stability across a range from 2.09 to 11.92. They exhibited salt tolerance and anti-oxidant capacity, even when the concentrations of NaCl and H2O2 were up to 4 mol/L and 1000 μmol/L, respectively. Notably, the SiQDs displayed efficient antibacterial activity without resistance by electrostatic interaction and excessive production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which would damage the bacterial cell walls and subsequently inhibit bacterial growth and reproduction. The minimum inhibitory concentration of SiQDs was 0.45 mg/mL against Escherichia coli and 0.25 mg/mL against Staphylococcus aureus, respectively. Besides, a fluorescence nanoprobe based on SiQDs was meaningfully constructed to sensitively and rapidly determine TC owing to the internal filtration effect. The limit of detection for SiQDs to TC was 0.0006 μmol/L with a linear range from 0.001 to 0.010 μmol/L. The probe's practicality was confirmed in spiked honey samples from different manufacturers with an approximate 100 % recovery of TC. Consequently, this research presents a promising avenue for enhancing the detection of TC in food safety and the development of antibacterial agents without bacterial resistance.

Silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) were developed to act as antimicrobial agents, which exhibited effective inhibition on E. coli and S. aureus growth by disruption of permeability and structural integrity of bacterial plasma membrane, attributing to the production of ROS of SiQDs. In addition, the SiQDs showed excellent selectivity to tetracycline owing to inner filter effect and were successfully applied to detect tetracycline in honey samples.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive and inappropriate use of antibiotics has emerged as a global issue, primarily due to their limited biodegradability in the environment. Additionally, antibiotic residues in food (Dan, Nho, & Tae, 2021) pose serious risks to dental development in children and gastrointestinal function in fetuses (Chao, Sun, Ye, Zheng, & Hu, 2020) and the physical health of humans. Tetracyclines (TC), one of the antibiotics frequently detected in food source (Liu et al., 2018; Wang, Yin, & Gunasekaran, 2023), have also been the focus of numerous assays (Durães & Sousa, 2019; Ye, Shao, Huang, & Yang, 2020). In recent years, an increasing number of advanced analytical methods have been developed for the rapid detection of TC, including high-performance fluids chromatography (Dong, Cheng, Sun, Zhao, & Kan, 2020; Liu, Zhang, Peng, & He, 2014; Phomai, Supharoek, Vichapong, Grudpan, & Ponhong, 2023), enzyme-linked immunoassay method (Korkmaz, Kuplulu, Cil, & Akyuz, 2017), microbial inhibition (Liu, Sun, Zhang, & Liu, 2019; Shao & Wu, 2020), mass spectrometry (Jiang et al., 2020; Rudek, March, Bauer, Pluda, & Figg, 2000), electrochemical (; Asma et al., 2020; Asma, Nasrin, & Mostafa, 2020; Asma, Sayed, Saeed, & Mohsen, 2013; Darabi, Shabani-Nooshabadi, & Khoobi, 2021; Ghoreishi, Behpour, Khoobi, & Salavati-Niasari, 2013; Khoobi, Attaran, Yousofi, & Enhessari, 2019; Liang, Wu, Gong, & Vomiero, 2021; Shekofe, Sayed, & Asma, 2020; Zhao & Zhao, 2023) and high-performance capillary electrophoresis. Despite the accuracy of these methodologies, the application is often hindered by the cumbersome operational, expensive cost and the necessity for specialized technical expertise(Zhang et al., 2022). Consequently, a streamlined and rapid detection methodology has become both crucial and imperative in the field of scientific research.

Nanomaterials are distinguished from other detection methodologies due to their straightforward preparation processes and exceptional sensitivity in detection. Research of next-gen nanomaterials like quantum dots (QDs) is increasingly prioritized over others because of their distinctive selectivity, electronic properties, size-dependent fluorescence, and tunable emission. However, the application of many QDs including CdS, InAs and PbSe is constrained by their toxicity (Chan, Liu, & Hsiao, 2019; Pandey & Bodas, 2020). This has prompted researchers to investigate silicon as a potential alternative. SiQDs, characterized by diameter of less than 10 nm, represent a class of zero-dimensional silicon-based nanomaterials (Wu et al., 2018) which can be utilized to detect TC owing to their excellent fluorescence properties, photostability and facile synthetic method (Chai et al., 2023; Chai et al., 2024; Li et al., 2019). For example, Liu, Z. P., et al use SiQDs and Cu2+ complexes to detect TC, where TC can competitively bind to Cu2+ through coordination bonds, effectively preventing SiQDs from the fluorescence quenching of Cu2+ (Liu et al., 2020). However, the intervention of Cu2+ complicates the detection process, prompting the need for more straightforward methods for TC analysis. Currently, various techniques have been employed to synthesize SiQDs, including chemical breakdown (the top-down approach), hydrothermal method (the bottom-up strategy), electrobeam lithography and microwave synthesis (Chen, Hu, Li, He, & Zhang, 2023; Fan, Zhang, Fu, Yan, & Ma, 2016). Among these, the one-step hydrothermal method is most widely used due to its high acquisition efficiency, reproducibility, and straightforward operation (Wang et al., 2020). Therefore, it is worth studying using a simple hydrothermal synthesis method to create SiQDs and applying them directly for TC detection.

Antibiotic detection is a vital measurement to ensure products are devoid of harmful antibiotic residues, while the efficacy of antimicrobial properties directly influences the level of bacterial contamination. The two processes are designed to ensure the safety and quality of the product, thus underscoring the importance of evaluating the antibacterial effect of SiQDs. Especially, SiQDs exhibit an outstanding feature of low toxicity, as the silicic acid produced in their degradation process can be readily excreted through urine (Chen et al., 2021), which makes SiQDs a candidate for the organism to resist bacterial infections without detrimental effects typically associated with antibacterial agents (Lovětinská, Holý, Exnar, & Veverková, 2016). The exploration into the application of SiQDs in the realm of antimicrobial research has progressively gained in depth and sophistication (Zhou, Chi, Chen, Pei, & Chai, 2022). The universal inhibitory mechanism of SiQDs is primarily formed by their cationic properties and the oxidation induced by ROS. This allows SiQDs to penetrate bacteria cells, leading to cell membrane leakage, and ultimately causing bacteria death through electrostatic action or the generation of ROS (Tian et al., 2022). This field of study has garnered considerable attention from the scientific community due to the promising potential of SiQDs in combating microbial infections and advancing healthcare outcomes. The pressing demand for a rapid and cost-effective TC detection method and the grave situation of antibiotics overuse caused resistant bacteria (Li, Mu, Xu, & Zeng, 2025) lead us to hypothesize that SiQDs synthesized through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method may possess dual functions of antibacterial and detection properties. They are expected to serve as effective TC sensors with high selectivity and sensitivity, while also exhibiting antibacterial activity against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria, attributable to their cationic properties and ability for ROS generation. This dual-function capability positions SiQDs as an ideal candidate for analytical detection and antibacterial treatment applications.

In this study, SiQDs exhibiting both non-resistance to drug and functionality for TC detection were synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method (Higuera-Valenzuela et al., 2024; Huang, Zhang, Dai, Miao, & Chen, 2024). The morphology, structural composition, optical properties and stability of the as-prepared SiQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and spectrophotometer, respectively. Notably, the antibacterial activity of SiQDs against Escherichia coli (E. coli) as a Gram-negative bacteria model and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) as a Gram-positive model was assessed to prove the antibacterial properties of SiQDs. In addition, the selectivity and sensitivity of the prepared SiQDs to TC were analyzed to illustrate the application capability of SiQDs as a sensor (Scheme 1 ). These findings indicate that fluorescent SiQDs may serve as a promising probe for practical applications in both antibacterial and analytical detection.

Sodium citrate as a critical component was procured from Macklin Co., Ltd. (China). Additionally, ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18 MΩ and phosphate buffer solution (PBS) were employed as solvents and buffers in the conducted experiments. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenylte-trazolium bromide (MTT), Nitrotetrazolium Blue chloride (NBT), tetracycline (TC), allyloxytrimethylsilane, and triacetoxy(methyl) silane were all sourced from Aladdin Co. Ltd. (China). The chemicals utilized in this study were all of analytical grade without any additional purification steps, ensuring their authenticity and consistency in the experimental process.

A comprehensive suite of analytical techniques was employed to characterize the materials in this study. Fluorescence (FL) signals were meticulously captured using a Shimatzu RF-5301PC spectrophotometer from Kyoto, Japan, with scanning parameters of 600 nm min−1 and slit widths of 5 nm for both excitation and emission. A JEOL 2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM), operating at 100 kV in Tokyo, Japan, was utilized to characterize the morphology. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted using a Nicolet iS5 spectrometer from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA, with KBr pellets as the sample matrix. The stability and charge distribution of SiQDs were examined using a Nano Brook 90Plus Malvern laser particle size analyzer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), equipped with an ESCALAB Xi+ spectrometer, was applied to probe the elemental composition of the materials' surfaces. The microstructural features of bacteria were further visualized using a Hitachi S-3400 scanning electron microscope (SEM) from Tokyo, Japan. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired using a D8 ADVANCE diffractometer from Bruker, Germany, with a Cu-Ka radiation source operating at 40 kV and 40 mA, emitting radiation with a wavelength of 1.5406 Å. FL lifetime was measured by the FLS980 Spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). Drug dissolution was accelerated at 25 °C with a UC-3480 ultrasound machine (Langjie, China). SiQDs were dried using an XS-3AH freeze dryer (Ningan, China). A Mini-15 K centrifuge (Ausheng, China) and DSX-24 L-l autoclave (Shen'an, China) were used in the synthesis and purification process of SiQDs. Dialysis membranes were sourced from UGGJ Trading Co. (Shanghai, China).

The SiQDs were prepared via a one-step solvothermal method (Wang et al., 2020). Briefly, allyloxytrimethylsilane (400 μL), triacetoxy(methyl)silane (600 μL) and sodium citrate (0.30 g) were mixed in ultrapure water (4 mL) and sonicated for 5 min. Then the SiQDs solution was transferred into a 25 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After being naturally cooled to room temperature, the clarified solution was filtering through a 0.22 μm micro-pore film filter to remove the large-sized particles. Ultimately, the SiQDs were dialyzed against ultrapure water to keep out small molecule impurities through a dialysis membrane (500 Da) for at least 48 h, and the ultrapure water was changed every 2 h for the first 8 h, about every 8 h for the remaining time to ensure effective purification. After drying using a vacuum freeze-dryer, the SiQDs were stored refrigerated at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC by the SiQDs
ce:section.ce:para: After diluting the SiQDs stock solution with ultrapure water, we moved the SiQDs solution mixed with a series of different concentrations of TC to the cuvette. Optimal fluorescence excitation at 325 nm was measured, and then emission spectra were recorded across varying concentration gradients. Some anionic and cationic solutions, amino acids and antibiotics were selected as interfering substances to test the sensitivity of SiQDs to TC under the same conditions. All experiments were conducted at room temperature. The honey was purchased from the local supermarket and the tetracycline content was analyzed using the standard addition method to confirm the utility of the proposed fluorescent probe. To ensure high confidence in the results, the fluorescence intensity was determined three times for each group.

Firstly, the frozen bacteria were inoculated into 4 mL of nutrient broth medium and then the mixture was incubated for 12 h in a constant temperature incubator at 37 °C at 180 r/min. We explored the antimicrobial activity of SiQDs with the gram-negative bacteria E. coli and gram-positive bacteria S. aureus as a model. The MIC value of SiQDs was measured by the 96-well plates method. The MTT test was implemented intuitively to observe the condition of inhibiting bacteria. Moreover, to further test the antimicrobial properties of SiQDs, time-dependent antimicrobial assays continued to be conducted. The initial MIC value for the SiQDs against E. coli and S. aureus obtained in the MTT test was used to assess drug resistance. All glassware and agar plates were sterilized for 20 min at 120 °C in autoclave in the experiment, and all antibacterial experiments and MTT staining assays were conducted solely on a specialized sterile workbench, and the ultrapure water utilized in the control group underwent sterilization in an autoclave, thereby ensuring that the procedures were performed in a sterile environment.

The morphology and size of the as-prepared SiQDs were viewed from the TEM image. Fig. 1a-b showed that the average particle diameter of SiQDs was 1.85 nm and the HRTEM image of SiQDs displayed well-resolved lattice planes with a spacing of 0.34 nm which may be attributed to the (002) facet of graphitic carbon. The crystal structure of SiQDs was further verified by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis in Fig. S1. The main peak was 2θ = 30°, corresponding to the diffraction plane of the (002) facet of graphite, which was consistent with the above TEM results. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which was used to determine the type and chemical state of elements, was very sensitive to the identification of elements on the surface of a sample. As shown in Fig. 1c, the XPS spectra displayed that the percentages of C, O and Si were 45.83 %, 44.01 % and 10.16 % in SiQDs, respectively. Fig. S2a illustrated that C 1 s had absorption peaks at 285.3 eV, 286.9 eV and 288.9 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CO and CO. Three absorption peaks of O 1 s spectra at 531.5 eV, 532.7 eV and 535.4 eV positions in Fig. S2b were allocated to the CO, SiO and CO. The spectra of Si 2 s and Si 2p in Fig. S2c presented three peaks at 102.9 eV, 103.6 eV and 104.3 eV, which could be ascribed to SiC, SiOSi/O and SiOH, respectively. The FTIR measurement was also used to further verify the surface functional cluster of the as-prepared SiQDs. As depicted in Fig. 1d, the SiQDs possessed a hydroxyl group characteristic peak at 3419 cm−1. The peak at 1584 cm−1 may be considered as a CC stretching vibration, while the peak at 1392 cm−1 may be considered as a CH bending vibration or a CO stretching vibration. The peak at 1278 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of SiCH3. The peak at 1079 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of SiOSi or SiOC. As shown in Fig. 1e, A typical absorption band around a center wavelength of 275 nm in the UV–visible absorption spectra could be attributed to the π → π* transition produced by the CC bond. Fig. S3 represented the fluorescence emission spectra of SiQDs at different excitation wavelengths (280–400 nm). It can be seen from the figure that the strongest emission peak at 420 nm was only obtained at 325 nm, which was the best excitation wavelength for the subsequent experiments. Simultaneously, the SiQDs solution was transparent under natural light and emitted blue fluorescence under the UV light at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 1f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC by the SiQDs

ce:section.ce:para: To determine the accuracy of the detection results of SiQDs, a range of experiments were investigated about the stability of SiQDs. As shown in Fig. S4a, the FL intensity maintained at a high level after 60 min of UV irradiation. Fig. S4b revealed that the FL intensity was basically unchanged in the pH 2.09 to 11.92 range, indicating that the SiQDs had distinguished stability in a wide range of pH. Finally, the tests of salt and oxidation resistance were implemented on the SiQDs in Fig. S4c-S4d, even though the concentrations of NaCl and H2O2 were as high as 4 mol/L and 1000 μmol/L in the solution, the FL intensity of SiQDs had no apparent change. The above results sufficiently indicated the superb fluorescence stability of the as-prepared SiQDs, which was beneficial for their applications in real samples.

Selectivity is a significant parameter in estimating sensor performance. The interfering substances, such as amino acid and other antibiotics (glucose, metronidazole, chloramphenicol, roxithromycin, amoxicillin, furazolidone, streptomycin, ciprofloxacin, doxycycline, arginine, alanine, lysine, glycine, leucine, phenylalanine, valine, tryptophan, proline), anions (C2H3O2 −, PO4 3−, F−, NO3 −, IO3 −, Cl−, S2−, Br−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, CN−), cation (Cu2+, Al3+, K+, Sn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Na+, NH4 +, Co2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+) were used to evaluate the selectivity of SiQDs for TC determination in Fig. 2a-b. Other components did not cause significant fluorescence quenching, indicating high selectivity of the fluorescent sensor for TC. To address the potential interference issue for SiQDs, we conducted additional anti-interference experiments by simultaneously introducing various ions, amino acids, antibiotics, and TC into SiQDs to observe changes in FL intensity. As shown in Fig. S5, the FL intensity remains relatively stable, indicating that SiQDs exhibit good stability and possess a certain degree of anti-interference capability. As shown in Fig. 2c, the FL intensity of SiQDs decreased gradually with the increase of TC concentration. The relationship between the FL intensity of SiQDs and the concentration of TC was analyzed in Fig. 2d. (F 0
F)/F 0 had a superb linear relationship with TC concentration in the range of 0.001–0.01 μmol/L with the detection limit at 0.0006 μmol/L, in which F 0 and F denoted the FL intensity of SiQDs in the absence and presence of TC, respectively. Compared with earlier studies in Table S1, the SiQDs fluorescence sensing of this method had a lower LOD than that of most previous works. Besides, to be emphasized, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon occurs immediately upon the addition of TC to the detection system as shown in Fig. S6, indicating that our method can rapidly detect the concentration of TC, eliminating the need for reaction time.

In the course of exploring the quenching mechanisms after mixing TC and SiQDs, a series of studies were carried out. In general, the fluorescence lifetime varied in DQE, while it hardly changed in SQE. As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence lifetime decay of SiQDs was nearly no change in the absence and presence of TC, which confirmed that the TC-induced fluorescence quenching might be mainly due to SQE, such as IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), rather than DQE. To further investigate the quenching mechanism, the UV absorption spectra of TC, excitation and emission spectra of SiQDs were discussed. As shown in Fig. 3b, the characteristic absorption of TC was located between 240 and 440 nm and partially overlapped with the excitation spectra of SiQDs around 325 nm, which is consistent with the characteristics of IFE. These results illustrated that TC-induced quenching was caused by IFE, excluding the possibility of FRET. To strengthen the validation of IFE, the absorptions of SiQDs and TC mixture were measured. The IFE mechanism was corroborated using Eq. S1 (Gauthier, Shane, Guerin, Seitz, & Grant, 1986)and the corresponding calculation results were shown in Table S2. The observed and corrected fluorescence quenching efficiencies were determined after the addition of TC at varying concentrations into a solution of SiQDs respectively as shown in Fig. S7. The findings indicated that the quenching efficiency of the IFE reached 78.14 % of the total suppressed efficiency, confirming that the IFE represented the primary quenching mechanism (Gu et al., 2017; Tong et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of TC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Honey from different manufacturers was chosen as the real samples for TC detection, and graded concentrations of TC were added to the honey samples to calculate recovery rates, facilitating practical application research. The amount of TC in honey was calculated using the above obtained linear equation. As summarized in Table 1 and Table S3, the recovery rate of TC in honey was around 100 %. The results showed that the SiQDs fluorescence probe had high precision and reliability, indicating that the SiQDs had potential in the detection of TC in real samples.

The SiQDs also responded to other TC antibiotics such as oxytetracycline as shown in Fig. S8a-b and chlortetracycline as shown in Fig. S8c-d with corresponding linear relationships. It is reasonable to postulate that alternative analogs or analogues can also be effectively employed for the purpose of food testing and analysis. When measuring the UV absorption of SiQDs, TC antibiotics, and one-to-one mixture solutions, we found that their peaks were at the same location, indicating they had similar UV absorption properties as shown in Fig. S9a-c.

Firstly, the 96-well plate method was used to study the antibacterial properties of SiQDs by adding different gradient concentrations of SiQDs to equal amounts of bacterial suspensions and incubating them together for 24 h at 37 °C in an incubator. As shown in Fig. S10 and Table S4-S5, the OD values of E. coli and S. aureus treated with SiQDs were noticeably lower than that in the control group. The MIC values were 0.45 mg/mL and 0.25 mg/mL against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. Then, the plate dilution method was used to further assess the antibacterial activity of SiQDs. As shown in Fig. 4a, the quantity of colonies decreased with a gradual increase in the concentration of SiQDs, which showed concentration dependence. These results were in accord with the data obtained by the 96-well plate method. As shown in Fig. 4b-c, the growth of E. coli and S. aureus was inhibited to some degree in 12 h when the bacteria were exposed to SiQDs at a concentration of 1 MIC. When the concentration of SiQDs was increased to 2 MIC, both bacteria were completely suppressed within 24 h. To estimate the potential of SiQDs to develop drug resistance in bacteria, the MIC values for 14 passages were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4d, the MIC values of SiQDs against E. coli and S. aureus remained almost unchanged after 14 passages, which suggested that the SiQDs did not develop resistance to E. coli and S. aureus. These results indicated that the prepared SiQDs had concentration-dependent antibacterial properties.

To visualize the bacterial damage caused by SiQDs, the morphology of SiQDs-treated bacteria was analyzed by SEM in Fig. 5 . The images of untreated E. coli and S. aureus showed a distinct and smooth surface, while the cell surface had numerous depressions and wrinkles after the SiQDs treatment. It showed that the structure of the cell membrane was in disrepair after contact with the prepared SiQDs. Thus, the internal components of bacteria would leak out, causing the death of bacteria.

To explore the antibacterial mechanism of SiQDs, the zeta potential was measured before and after the bacteria bonded to the SiQDs in Fig. 6a. It exposed that the initial potential of E. coli and S. aureus were - 28.76 ± 1.55 mV and - 26.77 ± 1.89 mV, respectively. After E. coli and S. aureus were in contact with SiQDs, the potential of E. coli/SiQDs and S. aureus/SiQDs were - 18.27 ± 0.92 mV and - 4.08 ± 1.25 mV. This confirmed that positively charged SiQDs were prone to binding with the bacteria through electrostatic interaction and eventually led to the death of bacteria. Furthermore, oxidative stress was one of the ways in which nanomaterials caused bacterial death. As shown in Fig. 6b, changes were observed when NBT was illuminated in the presence or absence of SiQDs, indicating that O2
− was photodynamically generated. It was proven that the addition of SiQDs would make excessive production of ROS, which would damage the cell walls of bacteria and inhibit their growth.

To address the challenges posed by antibiotic resistance and overuse of antibiotics leading to food contamination, this study successfully developed blue fluorescent SiQDs as antimicrobial and TC detection materials through a one-pot hydrothermal method, which displayed superb water solubility and excellent stability. Notably, these SiQDs showed the advantages of high sensitivity, powerful selectivity and rapid response to TC by IFE which was confirmed by the fluorescence lifetime decay and spectral overlap results and the calculated effect of IFE on the quenching efficiency. The practical application has been successfully verified with satisfactory recoveries in various honey samples. Simultaneously, the SiQDs as an antibacterial agent exhibited excellent antibacterial effects. Synergizing electrostatic interaction and ROS generation, SiQDs disrupted cell walls and ultimately led to bacterial death. Thanks to their unique properties and potential capability of interacting with food components, SiQDs are expected to be a promising candidate for developing innovative food detection systems. Additionally, SiQDs as bacteriostatic material have a wide range of application prospects for the subsequent medical, textile, food, environmental protection and many other fields. In future work, research can be focused on the following aspects:
(1)
Modulating the size, shape and surface chemistry of SiQDs to enhance their optical properties and optimize their suitability for specific detection applications.
(2)
Utilizing the fluorescence properties and chemical stability of SiQDs to develop novel sensors for the detection of pollutants, biomarkers, etc. in the environment.
(3)
Choosing a type of bacteria such as new coccine that is prone to infecting food as a bacterial model to evaluate the antibacterial application of SiQDs. In this way, SiQDs can detect antibiotic residues and inhibit bacterial growth in the same matrix.

Shuchen Pei: Writing – original draft. Xin Hou: Validation, Methodology. Yuting Chi: Investigation, Formal analysis. Wanlin Sun: Software. Fang Chen: Writing – review & editing. Kang Luo: Supervision, Project administration. Shuiqin Chai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by Chongqing Three Gorges Medical College 2023 University-level Natural Science Project (Grant No. XJ2023000204).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.141844.
